back: When the CPU requests data that is found in the cache memory, it is called a cache hit. The data is accessed quickly from cache without needing to access slower main memory.
When the CPU requests data that is NOT found in the cache, it must be fetched from main memory. This causes a performance penalty known as cache miss penalty.

front: What is LRU (Least Recently Used) replacement policy?
back: LRU replaces the cache line that has NOT been accessed for the longest time. It tracks the order of access using counters or a stack, assuming recently used data will be used again soon.

front: What is FIFO (First In First Out) replacement policy?
back: FIFO replaces the oldest cache line in the set, regardless of how often it was accessed. The first line loaded into cache is the first one replaced when cache is full.
Random replacement randomly selects any cache line in the set to replace when a miss occurs. Simple to implement but can replace frequently used data.

front: What is LFU (Least Frequently Used) replacement policy?
back: LFU replaces the cache line that has been accessed the least number of times. It maintains a counter for each line tracking access frequency.
The principle that recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again soon. This is why LRU works well - it keeps recently used data in cache.
The principle that if a memory location is accessed, nearby locations will likely be accessed soon. Cache loads data in blocks (lines) to exploit spatial locality.
Cache hit rate = (Number of cache hits) / (Total memory accesses). Expressed as percentage, higher hit rate means better performance.
Cache miss rate = (Number of cache misses) / (Total memory accesses). Miss rate = 1 - hit rate. Lower miss rate indicates better cache performance.
The additional time required to fetch data from main memory when a cache miss occurs. Includes time to access main memory and load the data into cache.
The smallest unit of data that can be transferred between main memory and cache. Also called a cache block, typically 16-64 bytes in size.
A compromise between direct-mapped and fully-associative cache. Cache is divided into sets, each containing multiple lines (ways). A block maps to any line within a specific set.
Write-through writes data to both cache and main memory simultaneously. Simpler, ensures data consistency but slower writes.
Write-back writes data only to cache initially, and marks the line as dirty. Main memory is updated only when the line is replaced. Faster but more complex.
